title: Copper will outperform gold and silver, could hit $30 in the new commodity cycle - Vizsla Copper CEO
description: Copper will outperform gold and silver, could hit $30 in the new commodity cycle - Vizsla Copper CEO KITCO
htmlContent:
<!DOCTYPE html><html data-theme="" lang="en"><head><meta charSet="utf-8"/><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1"/><title>Copper will outperform gold and silver, could hit $30 in the new commodity cycle - Vizsla Copper CEO | Kitco News</title><meta name="description" content="(Kitco News) - Gold’s unprecedented rise to record highs at $5,600 an ounce at the start of the year captured significant market attention, but now, with prices consolidating, one mining executive says investors should shift their focus to another metal.Craig Parry, Chairman and CEO of Vizsla Copper Corp, said in an interview with Kitco News that copper could emerge as the standout metal in the current cycle, potentially outperforming both gold and silver this year, as structural supply constraints collide with powerful long-term demand drivers.Parry explained that while gold continues to benefit from central bank buying and geopolitical uncertainty, copper offers the strongest upside potential due to a widening imbalance between supply and demand.“My top pick is absolutely copper,” he said, pointing to historical precedent and a dramatically stronger macro backdrop today.Parry noted that during the last major commodities supercycle, copper prices surged roughly sixfold, rising from about $0.70 per pound to $4.60. He argues that a similar—or even more pronounced—move could unfold this decade, driven by global electrification, the global green energy transition, and infrastructure investment.He pointed out that, unlike the early 2000s, when Chinese urbanization fueled demand, today’s drivers are broader and more persistent, anchored by the electrification of the global economy and the infrastructure to support the evolution. He added that even with growing economic uncertainty, the underlying demand drivers remain intact, supporting copper’s long-term outlook.At the same time, the industry faces a severe lack of new supply. Parry said that major producers are struggling just to maintain output at existing operations, while new projects face rising capital costs, permitting challenges, and long development timelines.He estimates that roughly $200 billion in investment is needed to meet future copper demand—but there are not enough viable projects to absorb that capital.This structural deficit underpins his long-term price outlook. While some banks forecast copper prices reaching $6 to $8 per pound, Parry believes the upside could be far greater, suggesting prices could eventually reach $20 to $30 per pound by the end of the decade if supply fails to respond.Gold, meanwhile, remains supported by traditional safe-haven demand and central bank accumulation, he said.Although geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East have temporarily curtailed physical demand—particularly from a region that accounts for roughly 20% of global gold consumption—Parry said he expects prices to rebound sharply once trade flows normalize.Meanwhile, Parry said silver sits somewhere between gold and copper in the current environment, with demand from the solar sector expected to absorb a significant portion of global supply in the coming years. Like copper, he added, silver faces limited supply growth, reinforcing its bullish long-term outlook.Despite near-term volatility driven by geopolitical tensions and shifting investor expectations, Parry said that the fundamental drivers for all three metals remain intact. He described the recent selloff across mining equities as an “extraordinary opportunity,” arguing that markets have overreacted to short-term risks while ignoring improving cash flows and strong underlying demand.Looking ahead, Parry expects a sharp re-rating across the mining sector over the next 12 to 18 months as earnings catch up with higher metals prices and capital flows return. Investors, he said, are already positioning on the sidelines, waiting for clarity before re-entering the market.As Parry positions copper as his top pick, Vizsla Copper is advancing its own growth strategy to capitalize on the expected supply gap. The company is executing an aggressive, multi-asset exploration program in 2026 focused on expanding its copper resource base across projects in Alaska and British Columbia. Its flagship Palmer project in Alaska remains the priority, where Vizsla plans up to 10,000 metres of drilling to expand high-grade mineralization and test new targets across the broader land package."/><link rel="canonical" href="https://www.kitco.com/news/article/2026-04-08/copper-will-outperform-gold-and-silver-could-hit-30-new-commodity-cycle"/><meta name="keywords" content="Kitco news,Gold,Silver,Platinum,Palladium,PGM,Platinum Group Metals,Metals,Precious Metals,Mining News,Crypto News,Bitcoin News,Ethereum News,Mining News,Interviews,Economic Reports,Forecasts,Central Banks,US Dollar,Charts,Tech Metals,Rare Earth Metals,Currency,Global Economy,International Policy,Politics,Bank Forecasts,Market Nugget,Mining Minutes,Roundups"/><meta property="og:url" content="/news/article/2026-04-08/copper-will-outperform-gold-and-silver-could-hit-30-new-commodity-cycle"/><meta property="og:type" content="article"/><meta property="og:title" content="Copper will outperform gold and silver, could hit $30 in the new commodity cycle - Vizsla Copper CEO teaser image" fetchpriority="high" width="1340" height="756" decoding="async" data-nimg="1" class="rounded-lg object-contain w-full h-auto" style="color:transparent" sizes="(max-width: 768px) 100vw, (max-width: 1200px) 1200px, 1340px" srcSet="https://images.kitco.com/img/height_361,width_640,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 640w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_423,width_750,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 750w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_467,width_828,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 828w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_609,width_1080,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 1080w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_677,width_1200,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 1200w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_691,width_1224,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 1920w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_691,width_1224,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 2048w, https://images.kitco.com/img/height_691,width_1224,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png 3840w" src="https://images.kitco.com/img/height_691,width_1224,format_webp,quality_75/icms/03b4dc86-7c13-45c3-8037-02da0dd43dc2.png"/></div></div><div class="relative text-base article-alias_articleBodyStyles__zkPMo article-alias_articleWrapper__atgO6" id="articleBody"><p>(Kitco News) - Gold’s unprecedented rise to record highs at $5,600 an ounce at the start of the year captured significant market attention, but now, with prices consolidating, one mining executive says investors should shift their focus to another metal.</p><p>Craig Parry, Chairman and CEO of Vizsla Copper Corp, said in an interview with Kitco News that <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a> could emerge as the standout metal in the current cycle, potentially outperforming both <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold">gold</a> and <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver">silver</a> this year, as structural supply constraints collide with powerful long-term demand drivers.</p><p>Parry explained that while <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold">gold</a> continues to benefit from central bank buying and geopolitical uncertainty, <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a> offers the strongest upside potential due to a widening imbalance between supply and demand.</p><div id="in-content-1" class="h-[280px] w-[336px] mx-auto my-6 flex items-center justify-center lg:hidden"></div><p>“My top pick is absolutely <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a>,” he said, pointing to historical precedent and a dramatically stronger macro backdrop today.</p><p>Parry noted that during the last major commodities supercycle, copper prices surged roughly sixfold, rising from about $0.70 per pound to $4.60. He argues that a similar—or even more pronounced—move could unfold this decade, driven by global electrification, the global green energy transition, and infrastructure investment.</p><p>He pointed out that, unlike the early 2000s, when Chinese urbanization fueled demand, today’s drivers are broader and more persistent, anchored by the electrification of the global economy and the infrastructure to support the evolution. He added that even with growing economic uncertainty, the underlying demand drivers remain intact, supporting copper’s long-term outlook.</p><p>At the same time, the industry faces a severe lack of new supply. Parry said that major producers are struggling just to maintain output at existing operations, while new projects face rising capital costs, permitting challenges, and long development timelines.</p><p>He estimates that roughly $200 billion in investment is needed to meet future <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a> demand—but there are not enough viable projects to absorb that capital.</p><div id="in-content-2" class="h-[280px] w-[336px] mx-auto my-6 flex items-center justify-center lg:hidden"></div><p>This structural deficit underpins his long-term price outlook. While some banks forecast <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a> prices reaching $6 to $8 per pound, Parry believes the upside could be far greater, suggesting prices could eventually reach $20 to $30 per pound by the end of the decade if supply fails to respond.</p><p><a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold">Gold</a>, meanwhile, remains supported by traditional safe-haven demand and central bank accumulation, he said.</p><p>Although geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East have temporarily curtailed physical demand—particularly from a region that accounts for roughly 20% of global <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold">gold</a> consumption—Parry said he expects prices to rebound sharply once trade flows normalize.</p><p>Meanwhile, Parry said <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver">silver</a> sits somewhere between gold and copper in the current environment, with demand from the solar sector expected to absorb a significant portion of global supply in the coming years. Like <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a>, he added, <a href="https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver">silver</a> faces limited supply growth, reinforcing its bullish long-term outlook.</p><p>Despite near-term volatility driven by geopolitical tensions and shifting investor expectations, Parry said that the fundamental drivers for all three metals remain intact. He described the recent selloff across mining equities as an “extraordinary opportunity,” arguing that markets have overreacted to short-term risks while ignoring improving cash flows and strong underlying demand.</p><p>Looking ahead, Parry expects a sharp re-rating across the mining sector over the next 12 to 18 months as earnings catch up with higher metals prices and capital flows return. Investors, he said, are already positioning on the sidelines, waiting for clarity before re-entering the market.</p><p>As Parry positions <a href="https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper">copper</a> as his top pick, Vizsla Copper is advancing its own growth strategy to capitalize on the expected supply gap. The company is executing an aggressive, multi-asset exploration program in 2026 focused on expanding its copper resource base across projects in Alaska and British Columbia. Its flagship Palmer project in Alaska remains the priority, where Vizsla plans up to 10,000 metres of drilling to expand high-grade mineralization and test new targets across the broader land package.</p></div><div id="spacer" class="h-6"></div><div class="flex flex-col items-start gap-7 rounded-2xl bg-gray-100 p-5"><div class=""><div class="flex"><a href="/author/neils-christensen"><img He argues that a similar—or even more pronounced—move could unfold this decade, driven by global electrification, the global green energy transition, and infrastructure investment.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eHe pointed out that, unlike the early 2000s, when Chinese urbanization fueled demand, today’s drivers are broader and more persistent, anchored by the electrification of the global economy and the infrastructure to support the evolution. He added that even with growing economic uncertainty, the underlying demand drivers remain intact, supporting copper’s long-term outlook.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAt the same time, the industry faces a severe lack of new supply. Parry said that major producers are struggling just to maintain output at existing operations, while new projects face rising capital costs, permitting challenges, and long development timelines.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eHe estimates that roughly $200 billion in investment is needed to meet future \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e demand—but there are not enough viable projects to absorb that capital.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eThis structural deficit underpins his long-term price outlook. While some banks forecast \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e prices reaching $6 to $8 per pound, Parry believes the upside could be far greater, suggesting prices could eventually reach $20 to $30 per pound by the end of the decade if supply fails to respond.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003eGold\u003c/a\u003e, meanwhile, remains supported by traditional safe-haven demand and central bank accumulation, he said.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAlthough geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East have temporarily curtailed physical demand—particularly from a region that accounts for roughly 20% of global \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003egold\u003c/a\u003e consumption—Parry said he expects prices to rebound sharply once trade flows normalize.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eMeanwhile, Parry said \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver\"\u003esilver\u003c/a\u003e sits somewhere between gold and copper in the current environment, with demand from the solar sector expected to absorb a significant portion of global supply in the coming years. Like \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e, he added, \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver\"\u003esilver\u003c/a\u003e faces limited supply growth, reinforcing its bullish long-term outlook.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eDespite near-term volatility driven by geopolitical tensions and shifting investor expectations, Parry said that the fundamental drivers for all three metals remain intact. He described the recent selloff across mining equities as an “extraordinary opportunity,” arguing that markets have overreacted to short-term risks while ignoring improving cash flows and strong underlying demand.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eLooking ahead, Parry expects a sharp re-rating across the mining sector over the next 12 to 18 months as earnings catch up with higher metals prices and capital flows return. Investors, he said, are already positioning on the sidelines, waiting for clarity before re-entering the market.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAs Parry positions \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e as his top pick, Vizsla Copper is advancing its own growth strategy to capitalize on the expected supply gap. The company is executing an aggressive, multi-asset exploration program in 2026 focused on expanding its copper resource base across projects in Alaska and British Columbia. Gold’s unprecedented rise to record highs at $5,600 an ounce at the start of the year captured significant market attention, but now, with prices consolidating, one mining executive says investors should shift their focus to another metal.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eCraig Parry, Chairman and CEO of Vizsla Copper Corp, said in an interview with Kitco News that \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e could emerge as the standout metal in the current cycle, potentially outperforming both \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003egold\u003c/a\u003e and \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver\"\u003esilver\u003c/a\u003e this year, as structural supply constraints collide with powerful long-term demand drivers.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eParry explained that while \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003egold\u003c/a\u003e continues to benefit from central bank buying and geopolitical uncertainty, \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e offers the strongest upside potential due to a widening imbalance between supply and demand.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003e“My top pick is absolutely \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e,” he said, pointing to historical precedent and a dramatically stronger macro backdrop today.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eParry noted that during the last major commodities supercycle, copper prices surged roughly sixfold, rising from about $0.70 per pound to $4.60. He argues that a similar—or even more pronounced—move could unfold this decade, driven by global electrification, the global green energy transition, and infrastructure investment.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eHe pointed out that, unlike the early 2000s, when Chinese urbanization fueled demand, today’s drivers are broader and more persistent, anchored by the electrification of the global economy and the infrastructure to support the evolution. He added that even with growing economic uncertainty, the underlying demand drivers remain intact, supporting copper’s long-term outlook.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAt the same time, the industry faces a severe lack of new supply. Parry said that major producers are struggling just to maintain output at existing operations, while new projects face rising capital costs, permitting challenges, and long development timelines.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eHe estimates that roughly $200 billion in investment is needed to meet future \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e demand—but there are not enough viable projects to absorb that capital.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eThis structural deficit underpins his long-term price outlook. While some banks forecast \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e prices reaching $6 to $8 per pound, Parry believes the upside could be far greater, suggesting prices could eventually reach $20 to $30 per pound by the end of the decade if supply fails to respond.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003eGold\u003c/a\u003e, meanwhile, remains supported by traditional safe-haven demand and central bank accumulation, he said.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAlthough geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East have temporarily curtailed physical demand—particularly from a region that accounts for roughly 20% of global \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/gold\"\u003egold\u003c/a\u003e consumption—Parry said he expects prices to rebound sharply once trade flows normalize.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eMeanwhile, Parry said \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver\"\u003esilver\u003c/a\u003e sits somewhere between gold and copper in the current environment, with demand from the solar sector expected to absorb a significant portion of global supply in the coming years. Like \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e, he added, \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/charts/silver\"\u003esilver\u003c/a\u003e faces limited supply growth, reinforcing its bullish long-term outlook.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eDespite near-term volatility driven by geopolitical tensions and shifting investor expectations, Parry said that the fundamental drivers for all three metals remain intact. He described the recent selloff across mining equities as an “extraordinary opportunity,” arguing that markets have overreacted to short-term risks while ignoring improving cash flows and strong underlying demand.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eLooking ahead, Parry expects a sharp re-rating across the mining sector over the next 12 to 18 months as earnings catch up with higher metals prices and capital flows return. Investors, he said, are already positioning on the sidelines, waiting for clarity before re-entering the market.\u003c/p\u003e\u003cp\u003eAs Parry positions \u003ca href=\"https://www.kitco.com/price/base-metals/copper\"\u003ecopper\u003c/a\u003e as his top pick, Vizsla Copper is advancing its own growth strategy to capitalize on the expected supply gap. The company is executing an aggressive, multi-asset exploration program in 2026 focused on expanding its copper resource base across projects in Alaska and British Columbia. Its flagship Palmer project in Alaska remains the priority, where Vizsla plans up to 10,000 metres of drilling to expand high-grade mineralization and test new targets across the broader land package.\u003c/p\u003e","embeddedMedia":[]},"urlAlias":"/news/article/2026-04-08/copper-will-outperform-gold-and-silver-could-hit-30-new-commodity-cycle","source":{"id":108,"name":"Kitco News","description":"\u003cp\u003eKitco NEWS has a diverse team of journalists reporting on the economy, stock markets, commodities, cryptocurrencies, mining and metals with accuracy and objectivity.
